name: Muggsy Spanier
abstract: Francis Joseph "Muggsy" Spanier (November 9, 1901 – February 12, 1967) was an American jazz cornetist based in Chicago. He was a member of the Bucktown Five, pioneers of the "Chicago style" that straddled traditional Dixieland jazz and swing.

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Waters
about_quotes.evidence: The transcript only contains the interviewer listing Muggsy Spanier as part of a band context ('With Muggsy Spanier and Morris Lane'). The interviewee, Benny Waters, does not speak or respond to this mention, providing no evidence of a personal relationship.

about_quotes.speaker: Mat Domber
about_quotes.evidence: I tried to emulate Pee Wee Russell ... and Muggsy Spanier. I mean those were the people that I grew up with as a listener.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mat Domber interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Manny Albam
about_quotes.evidence: I was a jazz clarinet player and baritone sax player with Muggsy Spanier... Muggsy had a big band at that time
about_quotes.doc_title: Manny Albam interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Marian McPartland
about_quotes.evidence: I listened to Sidney Bechet and Muggsy Spanier and Budd Freeman.
about_quotes.doc_title: Marian McPartland — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ed Polcer
about_quotes.evidence: The intensity of certain players, Muggsy Spanier and the beautiful intensity of Ruby Braff. Those are probably the strongest influences on me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ed Polcer interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: R.C.H. Smith
about_quotes.evidence: Muggsy was a very strong early influence. I had those old Bluebird, Muggsy Spanier and the Ragtimers. I learned a lot of basic things.
